title: Chapter 28: The Glass Tower
content:
*Unfollowed: The Silicon Valley Vampire Liquidation Guide*
*by Apex Shaw*
The Hudson River was a ribbon of black satin stitched with light.

Maya stood at the perimeter of the Top of the Standard, clipboard pressed against her sternum, headphones limp around her neck like a dead snake. The glass walls curved around her — floor to ceiling, corner to corner — and New York sprawled beneath her feet in a grid of gold and white and sodium orange, twenty stories of nothing between her soles and the city's teeth. The penthouse hummed with the particular frequency of money celebrating itself: four hundred voices overlapping in practiced laughter, ice cracking against crystal, the subsonic thrum of a sound system pumping ambient electronica at a volume calibrated to force intimacy. You had to lean in to hear anyone. You had to get close.

She was invisible. That was the gift of the black polo shirt, the laminated badge that read AETERNA AV CREW — MAYA R, the clipboard with its fake work orders and real frequency maps. People looked through contractors the way they looked through furniture. She had walked past three security checkpoints, nodded at two guards, and been waved through every time. Service workers were scenery. She counted on it.

Her fingers found the edge of the clipboard and flipped to the third page — a hand-drawn schematic of the venue's distributed audio system. Same architecture as the documents she'd pulled from Aeterna's internal servers in August. Same node topology. Same backdoor in the firmware update protocol. The seven transmitters Chloe had planted across Aeterna's San Francisco infrastructure — two in the server room, one in the broadcast uplink, four distributed through the AV backbone — were all reachable from here. She'd confirmed it ten minutes ago from the AV control alcove, her fingers dancing across the mixing board while the tech beside her stepped away for a bathroom break. Signal strength nominal. Latency acceptable. Chloe's hijack sequence was loaded and waiting, a sleeping dragon coiled in Aeterna's own networks.
Everything was in place.

Maya let out a breath she didn't know she'd been holding and tasted metal — the copper-sharp tang of adrenaline that had been bleeding through her palate since she'd boarded the plane in San Francisco. Three days. Three days until the IPO broadcast, until Chloe's code would hijack Aeterna's global stream and replace Vlad's carefully curated investor presentation with the truth — the feeding footage, the emptied bodies in Sublevel B, the thing that wore Alexander Vlad like a suit. Jordi's press network was standing by in thirty-seven countries. Marcus was positioned to leak the internal documents the moment the broadcast went live. Lucy had her exit planned from Site C.
Three days.

She scanned the room, ticking off exits on her mental map — the main elevator bank, the service stairwell behind the bar, the emergency exit past the coat check. Two fire escapes accessible from the roof terrace. The Hudson glittered beyond the glass, indifferent and ancient, and she thought about how many bodies that river had swallowed over the centuries. She thought about swimming.
And then she saw him.

Alexander Vlad stood at the center of the room the way a stone stands at the center of a river — not fighting the current, but forcing everything else to flow around him. He was surrounded by an orbiting mass of venture capitalists and senators and journalists who wrote the kind of profiles that made subjects seem like protagonists rather than predators. He wore a charcoal suit that absorbed light, no tie, collar pressed high against a throat that was too pale and too smooth. He laughed at something a silver-haired woman said, and the laugh was perfect — warm, generous, the kind of laugh that made you feel like you'd earned something. His eyes caught the chandelier light and held it, and held it, and didn't reflect it back.

He made eye contact with someone across the room, and even from forty feet away, Maya felt it — that double sensation of being seen and being sized up, intimacy and predation overlapping like wet paint. She'd felt it twice before: in the conference room at Aeterna's headquarters, and in the parking garage in Palo Alto. The sensation hadn't improved with distance.
But it was what was behind him that made her grip the clipboard until the plastic creaked.

There was a space. A void. A silhouette-shaped absence where a shadow should have pooled at his feet. The chandeliers overhead cast warm, buttery light across everyone — across the marble floor, across the bar, across the waitstaff carrying trays of something involving truffles and gold leaf. But where Vlad stood, the light bent. It curved around him like water around a stone, like physics itself was politely declining to illuminate him. One spotlight hit his shoulder and slid off at the wrong angle. Another raked across the floor behind him and stopped short, as if it had encountered a wall made of nothing.
He was a black hole in a tuxedo. An absence wearing a charcoal suit.

Maya's thumb found the edge of the EMP card in her pocket — flat, cool, the size of a credit card, capacitors charged and waiting. She didn't touch it. Just acknowledged it. Just reminded herself it was there.
She looked away first.

She made it another ninety seconds — mapping the last AV node near the east windows, confirming the signal path, checking her phone under the clipboard for Chloe's all-clear ping — before she felt the displacement of air that meant someone had stepped into her orbit.
"Ms. Reyes."
His voice was a cello played in a cathedral. Low, resonant, with harmonics that shouldn't have fit inside a human throat. She turned.

Alexander Vlad stood three feet away, hands in his pockets, head tilted at an angle that reminded her of a bird examining something it intended to eat. This close, she could see that his skin wasn't just pale — it was painterly, smooth in a way that defied the topography of living tissue. No pores. No texture. No evidence that blood moved beneath it.
"You've come a long way," he said.
Maya didn't flinch. She'd been rehearsing this conversation in her head since Palo Alto, and the script for the version where he found her was the shortest one.
"I go where the job is, Mr. Vlad."
He tilted his head further. A centimeter. Two. Like a compass needle adjusting to a magnetic field. "And what job is that, exactly?"
She held up the clipboard. Tapped the laminated badge. "Making sure people hear what they need to hear."

The smile didn't waver. It was sculpted into place, musculature moving with the precision of something that had studied human expressions from a textbook. But something behind his eyes sharpened — a lens refocusing, an aperture narrowing.
"An audio engineer with a philosophy," he said. "How refreshing."
A waiter passed between them, offering something on a silver tray. Neither of them moved. The waiter evaporated into the crowd noise like smoke.
Then Vlad leaned in.

The shift was subtle — six inches, maybe eight — but it collapsed the space between them like a controlled demolition. Close enough that she could smell him, and the smell was wrong. Not cologne. Not sweat. Something older. The air in a sealed tomb. The breath of a room that hadn't been opened in centuries — dry and mineral and faintly sweet in a way that made her stomach clench. Like the scent of flowers that had finished decaying and moved on to something else entirely.
"I know about the transmitters, Ms. Reyes."
Her heart stopped. She felt it stop — a single, systolic freeze, blood hanging suspended in her arteries like water in a pipe before the pressure drops.
"Seven devices in my AV network," Vlad continued, his voice barely above a whisper. Intimate. Almost tender. "I found them three days ago."
The noise of the party seemed to recede, sucked away into the void that lived in his shadow. Maya's fingers tightened on the clipboard until she felt the plastic buckle.

"Did you think I wouldn't notice?" His pale lips curved. "I've been navigating security systems since before your country existed, Ms. Reyes. Before the printing press. Before the concept of a 'security system' existed in any language you'd recognize."
He straightened. The theatrical closeness dissolved, replaced by something more chilling — a comfortable distance, the space a doctor maintains before delivering a diagnosis.
"But I left them in place." He studied her face the way one studies an equation — looking for the variable that doesn't fit. "Do you know why?"
Maya said nothing. Her silence was the only weapon she had left, and she wielded it.

"Because I wanted you to believe your plan was working." Each word landed like a coin dropped into a well — precise, weighted, echoing. "I wanted you to come to New York. I wanted you in this city, in this building, in this room — exactly where I can see you."

She could feel the EMP card in her pocket like a second heartbeat. Cold and rectangular and useless. You couldn't EMP a man who bent light around himself. You couldn't stun what wasn't running on electricity. The card was a child's comfort blanket against something that predated the concept of comfort.

Vlad's eyes drifted toward the window, toward the Hudson, as if he were admiring the view. "The IPO will proceed as planned. The broadcast will be clean — your friend's little hijack sequence will execute into a sandboxed environment I prepared for it. A beautiful piece of code, I admit. Ms. Chen is talented. But talent is not the same as wisdom."
He turned back to Maya.

"Your friend in Sublevel B — the one who's been feeding you documents — will remain where he is. The one in Site C as well. They've been monitored for weeks. Every document they've accessed, every door they've opened, every whispered conversation in the break room has been catalogued. I find your loyalty admirable, Ms. Reyes. Misguided, but admirable."

He raised one hand — the gesture of a conductor bringing in a quiet section — and pointed toward the elevator bank. Two men stood there, backs to the wall, hands clasped in front of them. Dark suits. Earpieces. The kind of stillness that came from professional training and the absence of internal monologue.

"I've already contacted the FBI," Vlad said, almost apologetically. "A warrant for your arrest on CFAA violations — unauthorized access to protected computer systems, conspiracy to commit wire fraud, the usual alphabet — is being processed as we speak. You have approximately..."
He checked the watch on his pale wrist. The skin was too smooth where it emerged from the cuff. Like porcelain.
"...fifteen minutes before they arrive."

The party continued around them. An investor laughed at a joke. A journalist spilled champagne on her shoes. The sound system pulsed with a bassline that Maya felt in her molars. The world hadn't stopped. The world didn't know. That was the most terrifying part — the normalcy, the continuity, the absolute indifference of four hundred people sipping cocktails eighteen feet away from a conversation about the end of everything.
Maya stood frozen for exactly two seconds.
She counted them. One Mississippi. Two Mississippi.
Then she did something Alexander Vlad did not expect.
She smiled.

It wasn't a desperate smile. It wasn't a mask cracking. It was the smile she'd worn in the parking garage in Palo Alto when she'd realized what Vlad was. The smile of someone who had looked into the abyss and decided the abyss was going to need a bigger mouth.

"You found the transmitters," she said. Her voice was steady. Her voice was calm. Her voice belonged to a woman who had not just had her entire operation dismantled in ninety seconds. "Congratulations."
She let the word hang.
"You think that was my only play?"

Vlad's smile held, but something behind it flickered. A candle flame in a draft. The expression of a chess player who has just captured a queen and noticed, in the same instant, that his opponent hasn't reached for the board.
Maya turned and walked toward the exit.

She didn't run. She didn't hurry. She walked the way she'd walked into the building — clipboard at her side, headphones around her neck, invisible woman in black doing invisible things. Each step was a choice. Each step was a declaration: I am not afraid of you. Each step was a lie that became truer with every footfall.
She felt Vlad's gaze on her back like a hand between her shoulder blades. Cold. Heavy. Searching.

He was trying to decide. She could feel the weight of his decision pressing against her spine. Bluff or not bluff? One move or two? The oldest problem in game theory, and she'd handed it to him with a smile.
The two security men at the elevator didn't move. Vlad hadn't given the signal. He was still deciding.
Maya pressed the call button. The elevator dinged — a bright, cheerful sound, absurdly mundane — and the doors slid open.
She stepped inside. Pressed lobby.
As the doors began to close, she pulled her phone from her pocket and typed with thumbs that wanted to shake:
**TRANSMITTERS BURNED. PLAN B.**
The doors were still closing — three inches of gap, two, one — when the response came:
**ALREADY RUNNING. TRUST ME.**
Chloe. Beautiful, terrifying, three-thousand-miles-away Chloe, who had apparently been running a Plan B without telling anyone. Maya didn't know whether to laugh or scream. She did neither.

The elevator descended. Eighteen. Seventeen. Sixteen. The floor numbers ticked down in red LED, each one a countdown, each one a small death of proximity. She could still smell him — that sealed-tomb scent, mineral and ancient — clinging to her clothes like a haunting.
Fifteen. Fourteen. Thirteen.

She pulled the badge from her lanyard and dropped it in the trash can bolted to the elevator wall. Useless now. She slid the EMP card back into her pocket. Still useless. Still there. Still a comfort.
Twelve. Eleven. Ten.
Her phone buzzed again. Jordi: **Press network is live on standby. 37 countries. Say the word.**
She didn't respond. Not yet. She needed to think. She needed to not be in an elevator.
Seven. Six. Five.

Plan B. What was Plan B? Chloe had been running something — what? Maya ran through the architecture in her head, searching for the node she'd missed, the backdoor within the backdoor, the route Chloe could have taken without telling her. Because Chloe wouldn't have hidden it from her unless—
Unless hiding it from Maya meant hiding it from Vlad.

Because Vlad read people. He catalogued every whispered conversation, every door opened, every document accessed. And if Maya didn't know about Plan B, then Maya couldn't give it away. Couldn't leak it through her expressions, her body language, her heart rate. Couldn't confess it to the FBI.
Chloe had kept Maya in the dark to keep her safe.

The elevator reached the lobby. The doors opened onto marble and brass and the cold blast of October air from the revolving door. The security desk was unmanned — or rather, the guard was looking at his phone, scrolling through something, oblivious.
Maya walked out through the revolving door and into the New York night.

The city hit her like a wall of sound and light and motion — horns and sirens and a thousand overlapping conversations, headlights streaking Washington Street, the distant wail of a freighter on the Hudson. The air smelled of diesel and roasted nuts and river water and rain that hadn't fallen yet. It tasted like metal and ozone and the sharp electric edge of adrenaline wearing off.
She had twelve minutes. Maybe less.

New York was vast and dark and full of places to disappear. Subways and service tunnels and apartments belonging to people who didn't ask questions. Jordi had a safehouse in Queens. There was a bus station in Chinatown that didn't check IDs. There were a thousand cabs and a million strangers and a city that had been swallowing people whole for four hundred years.

Maya Reyes walked south on Washington Street, clipboard abandoned, headphones in her pocket, phone in her hand, EMP card pressed against her thigh. Behind her, the Standard rose into the sky like a glass coffin, and somewhere on its top floor, Alexander Vlad was watching the place where she'd disappeared into the crowd, deciding whether to send his men after her or let the FBI do his work for him.
He thought he'd won. He thought he'd found all her pieces and swept them off the board.
But Maya was still standing. Chloe was still running. And Plan B — whatever it was, wherever it ran, however it worked — was already in motion.
The plan was compromised.
Maya Reyes was not.
